_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo/PipeDemo, identyfikatory testowe, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu M9. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant distinguishes an agent from a chatbot and from a workflow with a tool, based on who decides the next steps and whether side effects are produced.
questions.prompt: How does an agent differ from a chatbot and from a workflow with a single tool (tool use)?

options.text: An agent independently plans and executes a sequence of steps and decides on its own which tool to call and when.
An agent is a chatbot with a nicer interface — it only generates text and performs no actions.
An agent always calls exactly one predetermined tool in a rigid, unchangeable flow.
An agent differs from a chatbot solely in that it responds faster and handles a longer context.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes — the defining characteristic of an agent is independently planning and choosing the next steps and tools, which neither a chatbot nor a rigid workflow does.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: These describe a chatbot (text only) or a workflow with tool use (one predetermined tool). Review the screen on the three integration modes and pay attention to who decides the steps.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant correctly recognizes the meaning of the rollback control lever in the model of minimal control over an agent's actions.
questions.prompt: In the “4 control levers” model of an agent's actions — what does the rollback lever mean?

options.text: The ability to undo a change made by the agent, returning to the state before it was applied.
A record of all the agent's actions together with the time they were performed.
A gate where a human approves an action before the agent performs it.
Restricting the agent to the smallest necessary scope of access.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Rollback is the ability to undo a change — it distinguishes reversible from irreversible actions and determines the strength of the remaining safeguards.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: You are confusing the levers: B is the audit log, C is human-in-the-loop, D is permission scope. Review the screen on the four control levers.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant classifies a specific description of an AI tool's behavior as an agent, rather than a chatbot or a workflow, based on the independent chain of steps.
questions.prompt: A tool in the TEST environment reads the backlog from TestRail-SANDBOX on its own, decides which tickets are duplicates, merges them and opens 5 pull requests in the synt-shop repository — without being given a list of steps. Which integration mode is this?

options.text: Agent — it independently plans and executes multiple steps and selects tools.
Chatbot — it only replies with text to questions about the backlog.
Workflow with tool use — one predetermined tool in a rigid flow.
An ordinary full-text search in the test management system.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes — the tool itself chooses the steps (analysis, merging, opening 5 PRs) and different tools without a given scenario, which is a characteristic of an agent.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A chatbot only generates text, and a workflow executes one predetermined tool. Here the tool combines multiple steps on its own and operates on a repository — that is an agent. Review the distinction between the modes.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant selects the minimal access scope and the appropriate control gate for a task with an irreversible effect (sending an email).
questions.prompt: You delegate task Z3 to an agent: “Send the weekly report to qa-team@przyklad.test”. A sent email is irreversible (it cannot be recalled). How should you configure the permissions and control for this task?

options.text: Access to send the email + human-in-the-loop: a human approves the content before it is sent.
Full access to the mailbox (read and send) without approval, because it's only an internal report.
Access to send without a gate, but with rollback of the email enabled after an error is detected.
No access to email — the agent saves the report in the synt-shop repository and never sends anything.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An irreversible action (a sent email) requires an HITL gate before the effect, with a minimal scope limited to sending — exactly what Z3 needs.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: An email cannot be recalled, so rollback does not exist (C), and no gate combined with excessive access is high risk (B). D does not accomplish the task. Review how to choose the gate for irreversible actions.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant selects the minimal access scope to the repository, restricting the agent to a working branch with a review gate instead of writing to main.
questions.prompt: Task Z4: the agent is to “Open a pull request with a fix in the synt-shop repository”. Which access scope is minimal and safe?

options.text: Write access limited to a working branch with opening a PR; no write permission (push/merge) to the main branch.
Write access to the main branch, so the agent can immediately merge the fix without waiting for review.
Full administrative access to the repository, including deleting branches and changing settings.
Read-only access to the repository — the agent will describe the fix in a comment but will not open a PR.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Write access to a working branch + a PR provides a natural review gate, and the lack of permission to main keeps the scope minimal for the task.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Write access to main or admin access are excessive permissions with a large impact (B, C), and read-only does not accomplish the task (D). Review the principle of the smallest necessary scope and the role of a PR as a gate.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant evaluates which of the delegated tasks carries the greatest residual risk and requires the strongest control, weighing reversibility and impact.
questions.prompt: An agent in the PipeDemo CI (TEST environment) has four tasks to perform. Which one requires the STRONGEST control (HITL gate + full log + scope restriction), because it combines the greatest impact with the hardest reversibility?

options.text: Change the configuration of the PipeDemo TEST environment (potential write outside TEST, hard to undo).
Summarize 10 tickets from TestRail-SANDBOX (read-only, no changes).
Read the logs of the regression test runs (read-only).
Create an internal text report from the results (local write, easy to overwrite).

questions.feedbackCorrect: Changing the environment configuration has a large impact and a risk of writing outside TEST, and it is hard to undo — the rule of thumb “irreversible + large impact = gate + full audit” points to it.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Read-only operations (B, C) do not change state, and a local report is easy to overwrite (D). The strongest control is required by an action with a large impact and difficult reversibility. Review the classification of tasks requiring an audit.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies multiple real risks arising from granting an agent access to files, email, a repository and a test system.
questions.prompt: Which of the following are real risks of granting an agent broad access to files, email, a repository and a test management system? Select all that apply.

options.text: The agent may fall victim to prompt injection hidden in the content it reads (e.g. in a ticket comment) and perform an unintended action.
A wrong decision in the first step propagates through the subsequent steps, because the agent acts without pausing.
The agent may cause an irreversible effect, e.g. send an email externally or delete files that cannot be restored.
Granting broad access is safe in itself, as long as the agent uses a model with conversation memory disabled.
End-to-end encryption of communication eliminates the risks arising from actions performed by the agent.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The real risks are susceptibility to prompt injection, error propagation between steps, and irreversible effects. The control is restricting the scope and HITL — not conversation memory or encryption alone.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: D and E are misleading promises: disabled conversation memory does not restrict permissions, and transport encryption does not stop the agent's action itself. Review the access map and the sources of risk (M9.4–M9.5).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies the elements of minimal control and audit of an agent's actions, separating them from apparent safeguards.
questions.prompt: Which elements genuinely make up the minimal control and audit of a QA agent's actions? Select all that apply.

options.text: Permission scope — restricting the agent to the smallest necessary scope of access.
Audit log — a record of what the agent did and when, allowing the run to be reconstructed.
Human-in-the-loop — a human-approval gate before an irreversible effect.
Rollback — the ability to undo an applied change, returning to the previous state.
Granting the agent full administrator permissions, so as not to block it with a lack of access.
Disabling logs so the agent runs faster and leaves no traces.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The four control levers are: permission scope, audit log, HITL gate and rollback. Together they provide minimal control and auditability.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: E and F are anti-patterns: full administrator permissions increase the risk, and disabling logs destroys the audit. Review the “4 control levers” model and the role of the audit log (M9.6, M9.8).
